title: A real message has four parts: observation, feeling, need, request.
body: What I noticed (not what it meant). What I felt (not what they made me feel). What I need (not what they should do). What I'm asking for (not what I'm demanding). Most communication problems are missing-parts problems.
reflectionQuestion: What recent message of yours skipped two of the four parts?

title: An observation describes data. Everything else is interpretation.
body: "You were on your phone three times during dinner" is an observation. "You don't care about me" is an interpretation dressed as one. The first opens a conversation. The second starts a fight.
reflectionQuestion: What's an interpretation you've been treating as an observation?

title: Name the feeling underneath. Not the verdict it produced.
body: "I feel like you're being selfish" is a verdict, not a feeling. "I felt hurt, then forgotten" is the feeling. Sharing the feeling is vulnerable; sharing the verdict is armoured. Vulnerability invites repair; armour invites defence.
reflectionQuestion: What feeling have you been hiding behind a verdict recently?

title: Underneath the complaint, there's a need.
body: "You're always working" usually has a need underneath: connection, attention, reassurance, shared time. The need is more workable than the complaint. Stating it directly takes courage.
reflectionQuestion: What's the need underneath your most recurring complaint about someone close?

title: A request can be refused. A demand can't.
body: If "no" isn't a real option, it's a demand, not a request. People can hear the difference. Demands invite resistance even when the content is reasonable. Real requests open up real responses.
reflectionQuestion: Where have you been making a demand and calling it a request?

title: "No" is a complete sentence.
body: You don't owe extensive explanations for declining things. "I can't do that" is enough. Adults explain themselves to people who actually need the information, not to everyone who asks for any reason.
reflectionQuestion: Where have you been over-justifying a no that needed no defence?

title: Repair attempts matter more than not making mistakes.
body: Gottman's research: it's not whether couples fight, it's whether they repair. "I'm sorry, I started that wrong, can I try again?" is the most undervalued sentence in relationships. It works at work too.
reflectionQuestion: What conversation in the last week could use a small repair attempt right now?

title: A real apology has no "but" in it.
body: "I'm sorry I yelled BUT you were being difficult" is not an apology — it's a defence with an apology costume. The real one names the act, owns the impact, and asks what repair looks like. Nothing else.
reflectionQuestion: What's an apology you've owed that you've never given without a "but"?

title: Most people don't need you to fix it. They need you to hear it.
body: The instinct to solve is a way of relieving your own discomfort with their pain. Asking "do you want advice or do you just want to be heard?" is one of the most useful sentences in human communication.
reflectionQuestion: Who in your life have you been fixing when they wanted listening?

title: The conversation you've been avoiding is usually the right one.
body: Postponing has a cost too — usually invisible to you, often visible to the other person. Naming the avoidance itself is half the conversation: "I should have raised this sooner."
reflectionQuestion: What conversation have you been not having for too long?

title: Tone is part of the content.
body: What you say and how you say it land as one message. "Could you do the dishes" said warmly vs cuttingly are different communications, regardless of identical words. The four-part move works at any tone — but tone determines whether it's heard or repelled.
reflectionQuestion: When did your tone change the meaning of what you said in the last week?

title: Most conversations would improve if half the words were left out.
body: Over-explaining, justifying, prefacing, qualifying — each is friction that buries the actual message. The hard skill is saying the one true sentence and then stopping.
reflectionQuestion: What recent message could you have said in one sentence instead of five?
